title: DRS (Drag Reduction System)
description: How the rear wing opens to boost speed on straights
content: DRS is a movable flap on the rear wing that drivers can activate in designated DRS zones when they are within 1 second of the car ahead.
When activated, the flap opens to reduce aerodynamic drag, giving a speed boost of up to 15 km/h — making overtaking easier.
DRS cannot be used in the first 2 laps of a race or restart, and is disabled when yellow flags or Safety Cars are deployed.
Each circuit has 1–3 DRS zones, always on straights — never in corners.

quiz.q: When can a driver activate DRS?
options: Within 1 second of the car ahead

title: Tyre Compounds
description: The science of rubber: Soft, Medium, Hard, and rain tyres
content: Pirelli supplies five tyre compounds: Soft (red, fastest but least durable), Medium (yellow, balanced), Hard (white, slowest but longest lasting), Intermediate (green), and Wet (blue).
Teams must use at least two different dry compounds during a race — this is a mandatory rule. The strategic decision of when to pit and which compound to switch to is crucial.
Tyre degradation is a key factor: as tyres wear, they lose grip and lap times increase. The 'cliff' is when tyres suddenly lose performance dramatically.
Track temperature, driving style, and circuit characteristics all affect tyre wear rates. Some circuits are hard on tyres (like Barcelona), while others are gentle (like Monaco).

quiz.q: Which tyre compound is the softest?
How many different dry compounds must be used in a race?

title: Qualifying Format
description: The three knockout sessions that determine starting order
content: Qualifying has three knockout rounds: Q1 (20 minutes, all 20 drivers, bottom 5 eliminated), Q2 (15 minutes, 15 drivers, bottom 5 eliminated), and Q3 (12 minutes, top 10 shootout for pole position).
Each session has a time limit during which drivers attempt their fastest single lap. Teams often do 'out lap → hot lap → in lap' runs.
Pole position (P1 on the grid) is a significant advantage — the driver starts at the front and avoids the chaos of the midfield.
In Q3, the top 10 drivers must start the race on the tyre compound they set their Q2 fastest lap on (this rule can change by regulation year).

quiz.q: How many drivers are eliminated in Q1?

title: Pit Strategy: Undercut & Overcut
description: How pit stop timing can win or lose a race
content: A pit stop in F1 typically takes about 22 seconds of total pit lane time. The tyre change itself takes under 2 seconds with a crew of ~20 mechanics.
The 'undercut' is when a driver pits earlier than a rival. Fresh tyres give faster laps, and the driver aims to emerge ahead when the rival pits later.
The 'overcut' is the opposite: staying out longer while the rival pits early. This works when track position is valuable or when fresh tyres don't give much advantage.
Teams run complex simulations to determine the optimal pit strategy, considering tyre wear, traffic, fuel load, and weather.

quiz.q: How long is a typical pit stop?

title: Safety Car & VSC
description: How incidents reshape the entire race
content: A Safety Car (SC) is deployed during major incidents, bunching the entire field together behind the safety car. This erases time gaps built up during the race.
The Virtual Safety Car (VSC) requires all drivers to reduce speed by approximately 40%, but cars do NOT bunch up. Used for smaller incidents.
Safety Cars are often a strategic goldmine — teams that haven't pitted can get a 'free' pit stop since the time loss is reduced when everyone is slow.
The Safety Car period typically lasts 3–5 laps. Drivers must stay behind the SC and cannot overtake until it comes in, though the leader controls the restart.

quiz.q: What happens to gaps under a Safety Car?

title: Points System
description: How drivers and teams score championship points
content: The top 10 finishers score points: 25-18-15-12-10-8-6-4-2-1. The race winner gets 25 points.
An additional bonus point is awarded for setting the fastest lap, but only if the driver finishes in the top 10.
Sprint races (held at selected events) award points to the top 8: 8-7-6-5-4-3-2-1.
Both Driver and Constructor championships are decided by total points across the season. The team's points are the sum of both their drivers' points.

quiz.q: How many points does the race winner receive?
Who gets the fastest lap bonus point?

title: Flag Signals
description: What each coloured flag means for drivers on track
content: 🟢 Green Flag: Track is clear, racing can proceed normally.
🟡 Yellow Flag: Danger ahead — slow down, no overtaking. Double yellow means be prepared to stop.
🔴 Red Flag: Session stopped immediately. All cars must return to pit lane.
🔵 Blue Flag: You are about to be lapped. Let the faster car through within 3 flag points or face a penalty.
🏁 Chequered Flag: Session has ended (race finish!).
⬛🟨 Black and Orange: Your car has a mechanical problem — return to the pits.

quiz.q: What does a blue flag mean?
options: Faster car approaching, let them pass

title: Sprint Weekend Format
description: The compressed race weekend with an extra short race
content: Sprint weekends replace one practice session with a shorter Sprint Race (about 100km, roughly 1/3 of the full race distance).
The weekend format is: FP1 → Sprint Qualifying (SQ) → Sprint Race → Qualifying → Grand Prix.
Sprint Qualifying determines the Sprint Race grid. Standard Qualifying determines the Grand Prix grid.
Sprint races award points to the top 8 finishers (8-7-6-5-4-3-2-1), meaning extra points are up for grabs.

quiz.q: How long is a Sprint Race?

title: Aerodynamics & Downforce
description: How F1 cars generate grip through air flow engineering
content: F1 cars generate enormous downforce — up to 5 times their own weight at high speed — through wings, floor design, and diffusers.
Downforce pushes the car onto the track, allowing higher cornering speeds. But more downforce = more drag = slower straights.
Teams adjust wing angles for each circuit: more downforce for Monaco (tight, twisty) and less for Monza (long straights).
The underfloor 'ground effect' generates most of an F1 car's downforce, using carefully shaped tunnels to create low pressure beneath the car.

quiz.q: How much downforce can an F1 car generate?

title: Sectors & Timing
description: How lap times are broken down for analysis
content: Every F1 circuit is divided into three sectors. Timing is measured per sector to analyse driver performance.
A green sector time means a personal best for that driver. A purple sector time means the overall fastest of anyone in that session.
Sector analysis helps teams understand where they are gaining or losing time compared to rivals.
Ideal lap time is the theoretical best: combining a driver's best S1 + best S2 + best S3, even if they came from different laps.

quiz.q: What does a purple sector time mean?
